item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this so much and my favorite thing about this is that it's basically humans. We also go to war with each other once we realise the world has its limits and there is no more to conquer. And then we make religions.

I also love everything linguistic and enjoyed the worldbuilding very much. I'd give you +2 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the details of their societies growing and interacting, but can't help but feel like it fails to explore the potentials of their sociopolitical interactions beyond "the blue ones are in a cult and attack others". Similarly the details about the language are interesting but ultimately needless.

Also you have a broken link at the very end.

I upvoted, but I wish it was restructured to have a more compelling narrative about their peoples and their social development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

So… am I incorrect in assuming this is a species of RTS units?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree this could be better but rather than an excess of fluff(I think it's nice)I think it may be a lack of other stuff, I had to read through the whole article to get the basics, there should be stuff like alternative containment systems being considered, the "one's" speech could be better, as it is no one seemed to put much thought into it, definitely not saying it should turn out any different though. Also the description of the containment zone should be in the new procedures too, there'll be some redundancy but that's fine.

So in short I think fluff fine but consider the core too, it's a bit sparse and unclear.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Perfect, ta.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Woopsies!

Source:

All of the alphabet, derivations, pronouns, tenses, etc were customized and used with the program Vulgar, of which I paid for the full version.

Using the full version, I was able to customize pretty much everything, and the program simply took the alphabet I gave it and formed the letters into words. I changed a lot of the definitions myself, and even changed some of the words.

Anyhow, the pictures were all taken from what the program generated to visually represent what I programmed into it.

Drawing at the top drawn by the absolutely fabulous Sourbacteria

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: Images are good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have searched it, I must have glanced straight past it. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably because it's relatively easy to look it up on one's own: http://wanderers-library.wikidot.com/

Start reading here: http://wanderers-library.wikidot.com/orientation

Basically, it's a sister site to the Foundation wiki, centered around the concept of "The Wanderers' Library", a location that is frequented by members of the Serpent's Hand, but is open to all who respect and will protect its existence. It contains all the books ever written and all the books that will never be written, and is staffed by some rather interesting individuals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with the others here. It feels like for every paragraph that advances the story or presents something that is truly strange/anomalous, there are two paragraphs the feel like filler. Anthropological detail isn't a bad thing, but I need a reason to care first. (Also the interview felt pretty pointless, since it pretty much just restated what we already knew.)

Compare SCP-752, which packs story into a very small space through the perspectives of the various creators, the bizarre population, and the Foundation, or SCP-2978, which has a lot of the same concepts and story beats as yours, but has a weirder base anomaly and always keeps the story moving with new and stranger events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No one has actually given me a straightforward answer other than "a website" when I ask what the Wandering Library is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, the actual structure of the language WAS me, paying for the program allows you much more control. All the program did for me was arrange the letters into words.

If that leaves a bad taste in your mouth, that's fine, but I personally still consider it my own language. I created everything except the letter combinations, all it really did was save some time. But then again, that's only my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, that sort of makes this leave a bad taste on my mouth especially when said language is so heavily part of it.

Though, as a side-note, I'd support something like this on the WL more than on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, wow. I'd been thinking that the reason you featured the language so heavily was because you were so proud of your brainchild, but knowing you created it with a literal click of a button leaves a bit of a bad taste in my mouth.

On the other hand, I have a favorite new conlanging toy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with just about everything you said. While there isn't exactly fluff, there is extraneous information in my opinion, which leads me to no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About 4 hours, I used a program called Vulgar and paid for the full version.

It allowed me to customize the grammar structure, alphabet, phonology, morphology, pronouns, derivations, and everything else, and then it took the letters I decided for my alphabet and arranged them into words for me. Sort of cheating but not really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to admit, I'm really impressed by the details which went into the language. How long did it take for you to make all of that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally! This is on the main site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm forced to agree. An admirable effort, but it'd fit better on the WL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's more of a distinction between before being driven insane and not

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You weren't being rude, and even if you were, you are allowed to be! I appreciate the crit.

I had been toying with the idea of removing the language but I got enough feedback asking for more of it that I decided against it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, and I understand. I kind of agree with the title, but honestly, I still kinda like it. I do agree that the language thing was definitely an attempt at world building, but the information dump wasn't intentional. That being said, I do value the crit and I appreciate that you took the time to give it, and I appreciate you taking the time to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with the others. The amount of information provided for the anomaly's sake is too much for me. It's like, why would I even care what language they use? Sorry for being rude. It's not that I don't like the language or I'm against it, as I honestly think it's done well, but still, I just feel this incompleteness that hides within the details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a lot of respect for the tremendous amount of world building that clearly went into this - it was obviously a labor of love. As such, I can't bring myself to downvote it, though I feel (as others have already said) that it doesn't really work as an SCP; it's good writing that I think is just in the wrong place.

These little guys seem like a solid piece of a larger fantasy setting (like something from the Wanderer's Library), more than a stand-alone piece of fiction. There's a ton of well-written information here, but I found myself skimming through, looking for some kind of twist or punchline.

That said, even though this isn't to my taste, I'm voting neutral and rooting for it to survive; the foundation-verse is big and wide, with room for pretty much anything, and I bet enough people will "get" this to keep it out of the negatives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The amount of work put in here does not save this from being dreadfully tepid. The accompanying language document seems more like an attempt to make the article seem more impressive because you "wrote a language" but that accomplishes nothing if the article is lackluster. And it's lackluster.

World building isn't just something that can be forced. So much of this feels like trying to unnaturally create interest through information dump and it doesn't do anything to prop up the main article. It ends up feeling unnecessary and excessive.

Edit: Plus, I should also note that the title of this article is an instant turn off. There was a point where I thought that was funny, but then I grew up and now I don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for Smurf ghetto and Smurf language.

They are a peaceful species and have not been observed attempting to harm one another.

First, "They are a peaceful species" isn't as clinical as "They have not been observed attempting to harm one another."

Second, shouldn't this be crossed out, to acknowledge the creation of the warrior class? Or is this a distinction between -1 and -1B?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with the sentiment that this seems to be putting the cart before the horse on putting worldbuilding in SCPs. I think the material here could definitely work if there were more focus placed off the fluff details and more on a central core anomaly idea what-have-you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your conlang seems elegant, but it's given so much focus in the article that I was distracted from the actual anomaly. It all seems more like a world-building exercise with a few SCP-trappings added. I'm no-voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, I understand. I also realized i forgot to change all the -xxxx's. I am a dumb. WHICH I JUST FIXED AHAH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took a look at this in chat, not totally loving the sheer amount of fluff information, but I can respect the amount of time and effort put into that supplement stuff.

However, am downvoting for the numerous small errors:

  • "Translated texts written by SCP-XXXX-1 has" > either "texts written have" or "text written has"
  • "the artificial constructs created by said population (SCP-XXXX-2). / SCP-XXXX-2 are the artificial constructs of SCP-XXXX-1" > overly repetitive
  • The SCP-XXXX-1 sub-designation paragraph should go before the XXXX-2 one. It makes more sense from a logical and narrative reading perspective.
  • "in an isolated instance of SCP-XXXX-2 (henceforth referred to as SCP-XXXX-2A)" > this belongs in the description. Not having the sub-designation introduced in the description makes it harder for a reader to process all the information.
  • "society that SCP-XXXX-1 lives in appears to function similarly to feudal societies societies such as those of medieval Europe or Japan" > again, repetitive, and a bit run-on. I'd use "feudal systems". Also, medieval Europe and medieval Japan have some significant differences. If the main takeaway here is the caste system, I'd pick just one to reference and note that the "society functions using a caste system similar to that of [place] during [time period]".
  • "SCP-XXXX-1 have a typical lifespan of 215 days and will often seek out areas of sun to occupy." > use of "occupy" seems a little off here, especially since it's not clear to me as a reader how the fact about sunlight occupation is related to lifespan. Also, you might want to specify "sunlit areas" rather than just "area of sun" since that's a little colloquial.
  • "SCP-XXXX-1 use a form of basic currency for purchasing basic goods" > again, repetitive. Try substituting "simple" for the first "basic".
  • And so on.

As I stated in chat, this isn't quite my type of article. However, I do believe it could benefit from some more polishing up for professionalism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :DDD <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Best of luck to this awesome article, which definitely deserves to keep its place.

All the loves,
~Pecan
